channel.title: Posts on neon•Golem
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on neon•Golem

item.title: Feature creep, the project.
item.description:
neon.Volt - so what is it?

What is your favorite electronic gadget? I’m willing to put money on it being something like your phone or your iPad. If you think yourself clever, you might say an Arduino or something. As for me? It’s the power brick for my Steam Deck (followed closely by the Deck itself, I mean it’s basically a cyberdeck, but in contrast to the hobbyist ones you see, it’s actually good).

item.title: UART based control
item.description:
Communication established. Now what?

The last chapter details the pains of getting the silicon to respond to us. What needs to happen next is a two way communication with meaning. Since we’re working with a microcontroller, the first step is to establish a communication method that enables us to use the MCU as a bridge and talk to the target in such a way that we don’t have to hard-code every single thing. Up til now, I’ve set it to fire a command at the I2C bus on the push of a button and then report its’ findings over UART. The better way of doing this is to have the machine read the input from UART, parse the data into a command and argument form, then react accordingly.

item.title: The smoke test. Am I still an engineer?
item.description:
Am I still an engineer?

The question itself is pretty wild coming from me, someone working as an engineer, but hear me out. It’s been almost a year since I wrote any firmware or did any work on any microcontroller in general. This is then an excellent smoke test - do I still have what it takes? The premise is not too outlandish. Wire up an STUSB4500, tell it to negotiate 5V3A, 9V1.5A and 15V3A. A month ago, I placed an order with a Chinese manufacturer and received the boards last week. By default the IC does not negotiate 9V - which is incidentally the one I need, because that’s what the cheapest big capacity powerbank I chose to use does. For programming the device, I left a 100mil grid of testpoints that I intend to connect to using pogo pins. The 3x2 grid consists of 2 vias in opposing corners, a ground, a power and the I2C pins. Simple enough. An afternoon is all it should take.

item.title: Hello, neon•Volt.
item.description:
neon.Volt? What?

It’s a portable smart power supply that runs off of USB-C, utilises the rev 2 power delivery spec and is configurable over UART, but also comes with physical buttons, so you can use it without a computer. At the time of me writing this, it’s still in the system design phase, but I do have a preliminary spec for it and that goes as follows: 100W total power, 98.5% efficient at 20V input, 10mV adjustment steps, controllable via PC command line (so it’s scriptable) or via the knobs and keys on the device. I will not be able to guarantee that efficiency and the converter will probably not be that precise, especially with cable drop at higher currents. I can’t foresee everything that will go wrong with it, but I’ll do my best to make it work as best I can. There’s been feature creep. What started out with me adding hardware to meet the system specification ended up being “hey, if i’m doing this anyway, I might as well do more of the same” and that’s how I arrived at a full 16 bit user addressable terminal block, a user accessible ADC and DAC pins, a dedicated 3V3 rail and an adjustable output of 1 to 48 volts, a color 320x240 screen and enough flash memory to comfortably run Doom on this thing.
